latest.note: Camino Portugués da Costa; 16.9% of 531,000 total Compostelas in 2025. Second-most-walked Camino Portugués variant after Central.

trend.note: First year tracked separately by Oficina del Peregrino
Largest year-over-year jump of any major Camino

annualPilgrims.sourceNotes: All counts from Solvitur Ambulando which mirrors Oficina del Peregrino. Pre-2016 the Oficina del Peregrino combined Central and Coastal into a single 'Portugués' line in official PDFs; Solvitur Ambulando back-attributes Coastal walkers for those years using their own classification. The Coastal Way grew from under 3,000 walkers in 2016 to nearly 90,000 in 2025 — the fastest-growing major Camino by percentage. See docs/data-sources.md for the refresh procedure.

demographics.topNationalitiesNote: Per-route nationality breakdown for the Camino Portugués da Costa in 2024, sourced from Solvitur Ambulando. Notable: the Coastal route has the lowest percentage of Spanish pilgrims (21%) of any major Camino, and the highest share of Germans (11.6%) and Americans (11.2%) — it is the most internationally diverse major Camino in the Solvitur dataset. 'Others' (21.5%) not individually listed.
demographics.genderNote: Strongest female majority of any major Camino (61.3% female vs the all-Camino average of ~54%). The Coastal's combination of comfortable tourism infrastructure, flat terrain, and dramatic scenery has made it particularly popular with female solo walkers and female friend groups.

motivationNote: Per-route 2024 figures from Solvitur Ambulando. The Coastal Way has a slightly lower 'religious only' share than the Central route (41% vs 44%) — consistent with its reputation as the more leisure-oriented of the two Portuguese routes.

comparisonWithCentral.en: In 2025 the Coastal route saw 89,511 walkers vs the Central's 100,839 — only 11,328 fewer. Coastal accounts for 16.9% of all Compostelas and Central for 19.0%, together 35.9% — nearly tied with the Frances. The Coastal pilgrim demographic skews more international, younger on average, and more female than the Central. Most dramatic difference: Coastal has grown from 2,600 walkers in 2016 to 89,511 in 2025, a 34x increase — the fastest-growing major Camino by percentage in the history of Oficina del Peregrino statistics.

dataScope.en: This stats file covers the full Coastal route (Porto → Santiago) since Solvitur Ambulando tracks it as a single Camino regardless of starting point. However, this dataset release covers only the Portuguese section (Porto → Caminha ferry → A Guarda, ~130 km). The Spanish continuation through Oia, Baiona, Vigo, and Redondela is planned for a future release when the fragmented Spanish-side OSM relations can be integrated cleanly.

sources.name: Solvitur Ambulando — Yearly Demographics API
sources.notes: Primary source for 2003-2025 per-route counts and demographics. See docs/data-sources.md.

sources.name: Oficina del Peregrino — Annual Reports
sources.notes: Underlying source. Coastal was first tracked separately by the Oficina del Peregrino in 2016; earlier counts come from Solvitur's back-attribution.
